children.title: Regression Analysis with Humidity and PM2.5
children.summary: The segment discusses the incorporation of relative humidity (RH) into a regression model predicting PM2.5 levels. It presents the OLS trendline equation derived from the data, indicating a low R-squared value. The null hypothesis is stated as the RH coefficient being zero, suggesting that a simpler model without humidity may suffice. A bootstrapping method is introduced to estimate the distribution of the humidity coefficient by repeating the sampling process 10,000 times, and a histogram of the bootstrap results is generated using Plotly.

children.title: Incorporating Relative Humidity in Regression Models
children.summary: The segment discusses the necessity of incorporating relative humidity into a regression model. It compares a model that includes relative humidity to a simpler model that only uses AQS, noting that the former achieves lower error and is better for prediction. The estimated coefficients from the Barkjohn et al. model are presented, with the AQS coefficient at approximately 2.254 and the relative humidity coefficient close to zero at about 0.206. The null hypothesis is stated as the relative humidity coefficient being zero, suggesting that the simpler model may suffice. A bootstrapping method is proposed to approximate the sampling distribution of the fitted humidity coefficient.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Ready? So, is incorporating this relative humidity necessary? So, in all hypothesis, my model looks like this. Right? That the relative humidity has a theta to a coefficient of zero. Let's make an estimator again. Last way is a function. From my data, a sample my data, to the coefficient. Here I'm just trying to pull out just a relative humidity coefficient. That's the second coefficient here, right? My keys, my boot shop function again. Video: 1: File

children.title: Estimating and Analyzing Relative Humidity Coefficient
children.summary: The segment discusses computing the Theta2 estimate for relative humidity in a model, emphasizing the speed of computation after initial runs. It explores the significance of the relative humidity coefficient, suggesting it is informative and non-zero based on least squares regression. The null hypothesis states that the coefficient is zero, leading to a simpler model. The process involves bootstrapping 10,000 times to approximate the sampling distribution of the fitted humidity coefficient. A histogram is created to visualize the bootstrapped humidity coefficient, with lines indicating the original coefficient and confidence intervals.

children.title: EPA PM 2.5 Estimation Model and Bootstrap Analysis
children.summary: The segment discusses the state-of-the-art system used by the EPA for estimating PM 2.5 levels, which now incorporates data from Purple Air to provide more precise and timely air quality estimates in the US. A textbook related to this topic is mentioned, although it is not used in the class, and a blog by Josh Hudd is referenced for its engaging take on the subject. Additionally, the segment covers a bootstrap analysis of humidity coefficients, explaining that the bootstrap sampling distribution's center is near the original coefficient, and the null hypothesis regarding the need to adjust the model for humidity is rejected based on the simulated regression coefficients.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: about the actual date itself. About the truth. Turns out, actually, I guess we can jump back. This is actually what's done today. So these the state of the art system for getting the EPA. Estimates for PM 2.5 now use this calibration and have incorporated the purple air data. So we now use purple air and this exact model to figure out a better, more precise, more local and more timely estimate of air quality in the US. So it was pretty cool. This is the actual model that was put in production and they did very similar analysis to this. And I wrote a textbook which you're not using in this class, which I think we link in the slides in the notebook. It has a much deeper study of this particular case. And Josh Hudd wrote a really fun blog about this as well as he was trying to answer this question for figure out his daycare plans for the coming week. Video: 1: File

(We know that the center will be close to the original coefficient estimated from the sample, 0.21.)
By design, the center of the bootstrap sampling distribution will be near 0 because the bootstrap population consists of the observed data. So, rather than compute
the chance of a value at least as large as the observed statistic, we find the chance of a value at least as small as 0.

children.title: Calibration Model for PurpleAir Measurements
children.summary: A model is proposed to adjust PurpleAir (PA) measurements using nearby Air Quality Sensor (AQS) data, expressed as PA ~ β0 + β1QAQS. Karoline Barkjohn, Brett Gannt, and Andrea Clements from the EPA developed an enhanced model that incorporates Relative Humidity, represented as PA ~ β0 + β1QAQS + β2RH. This model is now utilized in official US government air quality maps, such as the AirNow Fire and Smoke map, which integrates both AQS and PurpleAir sensor data while applying Barkjohn's corrections.

children.title: Confidence Intervals and Colinearity in Modeling
children.summary: Repeatedly testing multiple parameters can lead to misleading results, as the overall confidence level may drop below 95%. To mitigate this risk, consider adjusting confidence intervals to 99% when conducting numerous tests. Additionally, a model is introduced that adjusts PurpleAir (PA) measurements based on nearby AQS measurements, incorporating relative humidity to enhance accuracy. This model, developed by EPA researchers, is now utilized in official US government air quality maps.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This brings up a concern you should have. So if you start doing lots of these tests for lots of different regions, you could trick yourself. This 95% confidence interval, if you do it lots of times, your overall the whole thing is no longer a 95% confidence test because you're repeatedly running that same color test. of expect that 5% of the time some things are going to go tell you the wrong thing. I'm that you're going to conclude that that. You know the thing is non zero when it's in fact zero. So there's some risk to that. So if you're doing lots and lots of confidence intervals checking many of your parameters at once, you should be cognizant that as you do more of those, maybe you should make your confidence interval like a 99% confidence interval to address some of that risk. I'm just saying bone-frony, there's statistical text techniques you can use to correct these that are out of scope for this class. But if you Google later ask chat GPT, it'll give you great suggestions to explore. Alright, last part, this was kind of fun. Colinearity. So we look at one more fun data set. children.title: Modeling Bird Birth Weight and Air Quality Calibration
children.summary: The segment discusses a study on the snowy plover, focusing on the relationship between egg properties (weight, length, breadth) and bird birth weight, proposing a linear model for analysis using a dataset of 44 birds to predict weight based on egg size, suggesting larger eggs lead to heavier birds. It also introduces a calibration model for PurpleAir measurements, adjusting these using nearby AQS data and incorporating relative humidity for accuracy, developed by US EPA researchers for official air quality maps. Additionally, factors influencing snowy plover survival reveal that bird weight cannot be accurately predicted by egg dimensions due to a small sample size, although larger chicks have a higher survival rate. Collinearity complicates data analysis, but regularization techniques help mitigate these issues. Correlation analysis shows that egg length and weight can describe one another and the target variable, leading to a predictive model for newborn weight based on egg properties. The challenges of analyzing a feature matrix with correlated features are addressed, introducing singular value decomposition and emphasizing the need for a full-rank matrix. An example is provided where egg weight predicts bird weight, suggesting a conservative model focused on egg weight and birth weight.

children.title: Modeling Bird Birth Weight and Air Quality Calibration
children.summary: The segment discusses a study on the snowy plover, focusing on the relationship between egg properties (weight, length, breadth) and bird birth weight, proposing a linear model for analysis. It presents a dataset of 44 birds to predict weight based on egg size, suggesting larger eggs lead to heavier birds. Additionally, a calibration model for PurpleAir (PA) measurements is introduced, adjusting these using nearby AQS data and incorporating relative humidity for accuracy. This model, developed by researchers from the US EPA, is now used in official air quality maps.

children.title: Modeling Bird Birth Weight and Air Quality Adjustments
children.summary: The segment discusses a study on the snowy plover, focusing on the relationship between egg properties (weight, length, breadth) and the birth weight of the birds. A linear model is proposed to analyze this relationship. Additionally, it introduces a calibration model that adjusts PurpleAir (PA) measurements using nearby AQS (Air Quality System) data, specifically incorporating relative humidity to improve accuracy. This model, developed by researchers from the US EPA, is now utilized in official government air quality maps.

children.title: Modeling Bird Weight and PurpleAir Measurements
children.summary: A dataset comprising 44 birds and their corresponding egg weights, lengths, breadths, and birth weights is presented to predict bird weight. The discussion emphasizes the relationship between egg size and bird weight, suggesting that larger eggs likely lead to heavier birds. Additionally, a calibration model for PurpleAir (PA) measurements is introduced, which adjusts these measurements based on nearby AQS (Air Quality System) data. The model, developed by Karoline Barkjohn and colleagues from the EPA, incorporates relative humidity to enhance the accuracy of PurpleAir data, which is now utilized in official US government air quality maps.

children.title: Bootstrapping Statistics and Calibration Model
children.summary: The discussion involves bootstrapping the all-theta statistic on egg measurements 10,000 times, organizing the results in a data frame with columns for intercept, egg weight, egg length, and egg breadth. Additionally, a calibration model is presented that adjusts PurpleAir (PA) measurements based on nearby AQS measurements, represented by the equation PA ~ 00 + 01AQS. The model developed by Karoline Barkjohn, Brett Gannt, and Andrea Clements from the US EPA incorporates Relative Humidity into the calibration: PA ~ 00 + 01AQS + 2RH. This model is utilized in official US government maps, such as the AirNow Fire and Smoke map, integrating both AQS and PurpleAir sensor data.

children.title: Building Calibration Models for Air Quality Measurements
children.summary: A calibration model is developed to adjust PurpleAir (PA) measurements based on nearby AQS measurements, represented as PA ~ 00 + 01AQS. Additionally, a model by Karoline Barkjohn, Brett Gannt, and Andrea Clements from the US EPA incorporates Relative Humidity into the calibration: PA ~ 00 + 01AQS + 2RH. This work is utilized in official US government maps, such as the AirNow Fire and Smoke map, which integrates both AQS and PurpleAir sensors and applies Barkjohn's correction to enhance PurpleAir data accuracy.

children.title: Calibration Model for PurpleAir Measurements
children.summary: A model is discussed to adjust PurpleAir (PA) measurements based on nearby AQS (Air Quality Sensor) measurements, expressed as PA ~ β0 + β1AQS. The model, developed by Karoline Barkjohn and colleagues from the EPA, incorporates Relative Humidity, resulting in the equation PA ~ β0 + β1AQS + β2RH. This correction is now utilized in official US government maps, including the AirNow Fire and Smoke map, which integrates both AQS and PurpleAir sensor data.

children.title: Snowy Plover Survival and Data Analysis Techniques
children.summary: Factors influencing the survival of Snowy Plovers indicate that bird weight cannot be accurately predicted by egg weight, width, height, or interstep due to a small sample size. Larger newly hatched chicks tend to have a higher survival rate. Additionally, collinearity in data analysis complicates inference tasks, but regularization techniques can mitigate these issues. Features such as weight and length show predictive capabilities, suggesting interrelation among them. A correlation analysis between egg length and weight demonstrates that these features can describe one another and the target variable, leading to a model for predicting newborn weight based on egg weight, length, and breadth.

children.title: Factors Influencing Snowy Plover Survival
children.summary: The weight of a bird cannot be predicted by various factors such as egg weight, width, height, or interstep. This is attributed to the small sample size of data collected. It is noted that larger newly hatched chicks have a higher likelihood of survival.

children.title: Collinearity and Predictive Features in Data Analysis
children.summary: Collinearity can complicate inference tasks, but regularization techniques help address this issue. Various features in the dataset, such as weight and length, show predictive capabilities, indicating that they are all somewhat interrelated. A correlation analysis between egg length and egg weight illustrates that features can be descriptive of one another and the target variable. The discussion includes a model for predicting newborn weight based on multiple features, including egg weight, egg length, and egg breadth.

children.title: Correlation in Feature Analysis and Model Relationships
children.summary: The discussion addresses the challenges of analyzing a feature matrix with many correlated features, which can lead to suboptimal results. Singular value decomposition is introduced, highlighting the necessity of a full-rank matrix, with a promise of further exploration later in the course. An example is provided using egg weight as a predictor for bird weight, demonstrating that heavier eggs are associated with heavier birds. A conservative model is suggested, concentrating on egg weight and birth weight, and the relationship between these features is emphasized. Data on the Snowy Plover bird is also mentioned, indicating that larger newly hatched chicks have a higher survival rate.

children.title: Correlation in Feature Analysis and Model Relationships
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the challenges of analyzing a feature matrix with many correlated features, indicating that the analysis may not yield great results. The concept of singular value decomposition is introduced, emphasizing the importance of having a full-rank matrix, although this topic will be covered in more detail later in the course. An example is provided using egg weight as a predictor for bird weight, illustrating that heavier eggs likely lead to heavier birds. A more conservative model is proposed, focusing solely on egg weight and birth weight, highlighting the relationship between these features. Additionally, data on the Snowy Plover bird is mentioned, noting that larger newly hatched chicks have a higher survival rate.

children.title: Modeling Birth Weight from Egg Characteristics
children.summary: A model is developed to estimate birth weight based on egg weight, length, and breadth, utilizing bootstrapping techniques with 10,000 samples. The analysis shows that egg weight is a significant predictor of birth weight, with other features also contributing. This relationship is supported by biological reasoning, indicating that if an egg weighs nothing, the bird's weight would also be zero. The data focuses on the Snowy Plover, an endangered species, aiming to understand the impact of egg size on newly-hatched chicks.

children.title: Modeling Birth Weight from Egg Characteristics
children.summary: A model is developed to estimate birth weight based on egg weight, egg length, and egg breadth, using bootstrapping techniques with 10,000 samples. The coefficients indicate that egg weight significantly predicts birth weight, while the other features also contribute to the model. The relationship is grounded in biological reasoning, suggesting that if an egg weighs nothing, the bird's weight would also be zero. The data pertains to the Snowy Plover, an endangered bird species, with the goal of understanding how egg size affects newly-hatched chicks.

The Data
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
The Snowy Plover is a tiny bird that lives on the coast in parts of California and elsewhere. It is so small that it is vulnerable to many predators and to people and dogs
that don't look where they are stepping when they go to the beach. It is considered endangered in many parts of the US.
The data are about the eggs and newly-hatched chicks of the Snowy Plover. Here's a parent bird and some eggs.
The data were collected at the Point Reyes National Seashore by a former student at Berkeley. The goal was to see how the size of an egg could be used to predict the
weight of the resulting chick. The bigger the newly-hatched chick, the more likely it is to survive.

children.title: Predicting Bird Weight from Egg Size and Modeling Relationships
children.summary: The segment discusses the correlation between the weight of Snowy Plover eggs and the resulting chicks, indicating that egg weight can estimate chick weight, crucial for survival. It presents a model formula fo(x) = 00 + 01egg_length + 02egg_breadth + 03egg_weight + €, focusing on predicting bird weight from egg characteristics. The analysis reveals issues with collinearity among features, leading to non-significant coefficients despite individual significance. It emphasizes the assumptions of unobservable parameters and random error in the model. Additionally, challenges in parameter estimation in linear regression are addressed, including the interpretation of slope coefficients and the effects of small data changes.

children.title: Predicting Bird Weight from Egg Size
children.summary: The segment discusses the relationship between the weight of Snowy Plover eggs and the weight of the resulting chicks. It highlights that the egg's weight can provide a good estimate of the bird's weight after hatching, with the expectation that the ratio of bird weight to egg weight will be less than one but greater than zero. The data collected at Point Reyes National Seashore aims to determine how egg size can predict chick weight, which is vital for the chick's survival. Additionally, egg and bird weights are measured in grams, with a standard paper clip as a reference for weight.

The Data
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
The Snowy Plover is a tiny bird that lives on the coast in parts of California and elsewhere. It is so small that it is vulnerable to many predators and to people and dogs
that don't look where they are stepping when they go to the beach. It is considered endangered in many parts of the US.
The data are about the eggs and newly-hatched chicks of the Snowy Plover. Here's a parent bird and some eggs.
The data were collected at the Point Reyes National Seashore by a former student at Berkeley. The goal was to see how the size of an egg could be used to predict the
weight of the resulting chick. The bigger the newly-hatched chick, the more likely it is to survive.

children.title: Modeling Egg and Bird Weight Relationships
children.summary: The segment discusses a model to predict bird weight based on egg characteristics such as weight, length, and breadth. It introduces the model formula fo(x) = 00 + 01egg_length + 02egg_breadth + 03egg_weight + €, where the parameters are unobservable and estimated, and the random error is assumed to have an expectation of 0. A dataset of snowy plover eggs is analyzed, showing the relationship between egg measurements and bird weight.

children.title: Collinearity in Feature Analysis and Model Setup
children.summary: The discussion covers the rejection of the null hypothesis in a specific setup, highlighting issues with collinear features. It explains that while individual features may show significance, combining them can lead to non-significant coefficients due to collinearity, resulting in a non-full-rank matrix. A review of the model setup is presented, where egg characteristics (length, breadth, weight) are used to predict bird weight, emphasizing the assumptions of unobservable parameters and random error in the model.

leaf_chunk.text:

children.title: Linear Regression Parameter Estimation and Bootstrapping
children.summary: Discussion on the challenges of parameter inference in linear regression, particularly regarding the interpretation of slope coefficients and the impact of small data changes on estimates. A worked example demonstrates fitting a linear model to predict bird weight based on egg characteristics, presenting the intercept and coefficients along with the RMSE. The segment introduces bootstrapping to obtain 95% confidence intervals for the parameters, emphasizing the unobservable nature of true parameters and random error.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I have all those problems with inversion and again now you know that I now have problems with the parameter inference as well. Okay. Talk about this. Yeah, so this is the punchline here as a cleanery when features can be predicted accurately by a linear function of the other features. The features have a query with others, which we saw in the correlation plot. These slopes coefficients are hard to interpret using the analysis we have. We've already discussed it's not invertible. Yeah, and then small chat is a very small changes in data sample can lead to big changes in the estimated slopes. Video: 1: File

children.title: Regularization and Model Parameterization Discussion
children.summary: Regularization can enhance prediction stability, although aggressive regularization may not always be necessary during inference. A simple example illustrates that two functions can be parameterized in various ways, leading to multiple equivalent models. This variability complicates data interpretation. The segment also discusses estimating unobservable parameters and random errors in a linear regression context, with a demonstration of fitting a model to bird weight data using egg weight, length, and breadth as features. The results include the model's coefficients and RMSE, and an introduction to bootstrapping for obtaining confidence intervals for the parameters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Regularization can help with some of this, but typically, we're doing this inference, you may not actually use aggressive regularization. That's more for improving prediction stability. Good example. Yeah, we'll skip it. There's an example of the slides for those who are watching later. You can think through a simple setup where you have two exact functions of each other, and then you can choose any model parameterization where you put all the way on one feature, all the way on the other feature. They're both equivalent, and that means that I could put all the way to one. Zero on the other I can put too much weight on one and subtract off the other so there's a wide range of of theta that I could use to express the same model. So it makes it hard to infer what those data are. Alright, so we did the demo. think we cannot but... Video: 1: File

leaf_chunk.audio: Regularization can help with some of this, but typically, we're doing this inference, you may not actually use aggressive regularization. That's more for improving prediction stability. Good example. Yeah, we'll skip it. There's an example of the slides for those who are watching later. You can think through a simple setup where you have two exact functions of each other, and then you can choose any model parameterization where you put all the way on one feature, all the way on the other feature. They're both equivalent, and that means that I could put all the way to one. Zero on the other I can put too much weight on one and subtract off the other so there's a wide range of of theta that I could use to express the same model. So it makes it hard to infer what those data are. Alright, so we did the demo. think we cannot but...

children.title: Importance of Assumptions in Regression Analysis
children.summary: The regression model relies on certain assumptions, particularly the existence of a true linear model. If these assumptions are violated, especially when fitting unusual data, the analysis may yield meaningless conclusions. This discussion connects to earlier content on bootstrapping.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: All right, yes, this is assumptions matter. We are assuming the regression model holds. This is important so I'm assuming there is a true linear model and I'm building my analysis around that. And this is not true if I'm fitting very weird data. I may not be able to get meaningful conclusions. We talk about the assumptions just links to the earlier slide for bootstrap. Video: 1: File

children.title: Bootstrap Sample Size and Upcoming Topics
children.summary: A larger sample size is recommended for bootstrapping to obtain good estimates, ideally in the hundreds or thousands, especially with skewed data. There will be a break next week, with the first half off and a return in the second half to discuss new systems. The off-sour session will be rescheduled.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It is important you check these things. Your sample size is large. It's 34 large, not really. So the bootstrap is trying out the best way to estimate that. You probably want a bigger sample. So kind of thinking of the order of hundreds. Good estimates. If your data is very well behaved, you know? What are tens of data could be good, but in general if you have interesting data, hundreds, thousands, better free boots jump. We talk about skewed statistics you need to watch out for that as well. Alright, there's some bonus material I won't cover it will stop here again slightly early. I will be out next week, the first half, I'll be out back the second half. We'll be jumping into sequels, so we're talking about a whole new set of systems next week, which would be quite a lot of fun. But I won't have off-sour, so I'll have to reschedule that to later in the week. Thank you all and see you. Thursday. Video: 1: File

You probably want a bigger sample. So kind of thinking of the order of hundreds. Good estimates. If your data is very well behaved, you know? What are tens of data could be good, but in general if you have interesting data, hundreds, thousands, better free boots jump. We talk about skewed statistics you need to watch out for that as well. Alright, there's some bonus material I won't cover it will stop here again slightly early. I will be out next week, the first half, I'll be out back the second half.
